turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has our opinion in case 16-54, Esquivel-Quintana versus Sessions.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit. The Immigration and Nationality Act provides that aliens maybe removed from the United States if they are convicted of an aggravated felony. In 1996, Congress amended the act to make sexual abuse of a minor an aggravated felony. This case is about whether a conviction for statutory rape under California Law qualifies as sexual abuse of a minor. Petition of Juan Esquivel-Quintana was convicted of statutory rape in California where the age of consent is 18. The Department of Homeland Security began removal proceedings against him contending that his conviction qualified as sexual abuse of a minor. An immigration judge agreed and ordered that he be removed to Mexico. Eventually, the Sixth Circuit upheld that order. In an opinion filed with the clerk today, we reverse. We hold that in the context of statutory rape offenses that criminalized sexual intercourse based solely on the age of the participants, the federal definition of sexual abuse of a minor requires that the victim be younger than 16. Because the California statute at issue in this case sets an age of consent older than 16, a conviction under that statute does not qualify as sexual abuse of a minor. In reaching this conclusion, we used the normal tools of statutory interpretation to interpret the phrase “sexual abuse of a minor,” as of the time it was added to the Immigration and Nationality Act. We start with the text. Reliable dictionaries from 1996 provide evidence that the generic age of consent at that time was 16. Relevant context confirms that understanding. A closely related federal statute, 18 U.S.C. 2243, provides the only definition of sexual abuse of a minor in the United States Code. Congress amended that statute to protect children under 16 in the same legislation that added sexual abuse of a minor to the Immigration and Nationality Act. This suggests that Congress understood the phrase “sexual abuse of a minor” to protect the victims under but not over 16. In addition, a significant majority of state set the age of consent at 16 in 1996, 16 was also the default age suggested by the Model Penal Code. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Sixth Circuit. The decision of the Court is unanimous. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
